questions.question: What is the most common cause of leukocoria in children under 1 year of age?
questions.explanation: Congenital cataract is the most common cause of leukocoria in infants, surpassing retinoblastoma in overall frequency.

questions.question: What ophthalmoscopic finding typically characterizes endophytic retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Endophytic retinoblastoma grows towards the vitreous, presenting as a white mass with calcifications visible in the fundus.

questions.question: What is the first imaging study indicated when retinoblastoma is suspected?
questions.explanation: B-mode ocular ultrasound is the first choice because it detects intraocular calcifications and masses without ionizing radiation.

questions.question: What gene is mutated in most hereditary retinoblastomas?
questions.explanation: The RB1 gene is a tumor suppressor gene whose biallelic mutation is the fundamental cause of hereditary and sporadic retinoblastoma.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for advanced unilateral intraocular retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Enucleation is the treatment of choice in advanced unilateral intraocular retinoblastoma when there is no possibility of preserving useful vision.

questions.question: What clinical sign differentiates retinoblastoma from Coats disease?
questions.explanation: Intraocular calcifications are typical of retinoblastoma and are not observed in Coats' disease, which presents with telangiectasias and exudates.

questions.question: What is the most common age at which retinoblastoma is diagnosed?
questions.explanation: Retinoblastoma is typically diagnosed before the age of 5 years, with a peak incidence between the first and second year of life. It is very rare after 5 years.

questions.question: What clinical sign guides the diagnosis of retinoblastoma in a child under 5 years of age?
questions.explanation: Leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is the most characteristic and common sign of retinoblastoma in young children. Red eye and exophthalmos are less specific.

questions.question: What is the most common inherited form of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: The hereditary form of retinoblastoma is due to a germline mutation in the RB1 gene, which is transmitted in an autosomal dominant manner. Somatic mutations are sporadic.

questions.question: Which imaging test is preferred to evaluate intraocular retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: B-mode ocular ultrasound is the preferred initial test to detect intraocular masses with calcifications in retinoblastoma. MRI is used for extracular extension.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for advanced unilateral intraocular retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Enucleation remains the standard treatment for advanced unilateral intraocular retinoblastoma (groups D and E), especially when there is no useful vision and there is a high risk of extension.

questions.question: What is the main prognostic factor in retinoblastoma?
options: Age at diagnosis greater than 3 years
questions.explanation: Extraocular extension (invasion of the optic nerve, choroid or sclera) is the most important prognostic factor, associated with a higher risk of metastasis and worse survival.

questions.question: What type of inheritance does the germline mutation of the RB1 gene present?
questions.explanation: The germline mutation of the RB1 gene follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern with high penetrance, which predisposes to hereditary retinoblastoma.

questions.question: What is the main function of the protein encoded by the RB1 gene?
questions.explanation: The pRB protein acts as a tumor suppressor by regulating the G1/S transition of the cell cycle by inhibiting E2F transcription factors.

questions.question: On which chromosome is the RB1 gene located?
questions.explanation: The RB1 gene is located on the long arm of chromosome 13, specifically in band 13q14, and its loss or mutation is associated with retinoblastoma.

questions.question: What is the classic clinical sign of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is the most common and characteristic sign of retinoblastoma, visible to the naked eye or in flash photographs.
hashtags: Basic and Clinical Science Course AAO 2023

questions.question: Which imaging study is preferred for the initial evaluation of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Magnetic resonance imaging is the study of choice to evaluate extension of retinoblastoma, optic nerve involvement and intracranial involvement, avoiding ionizing radiation.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for advanced unilateral intraocular retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Enucleation remains the standard treatment for advanced unilateral intraocular retinoblastoma (groups D/E), since it reduces the risk of metastasis and allows for a complete histopathological study.

questions.question: What is the most frequently mutated gene in the inherited form of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: The hereditary form of retinoblastoma is associated with germline mutations in the RB1 gene, a tumor suppressor gene located on chromosome 13q14.

questions.question: What clinical feature is typical of bilateral hereditary retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) and strabismus are the most common clinical manifestations of retinoblastoma, especially in the bilateral and multifocal form.

questions.question: What inheritance pattern does bilateral hereditary retinoblastoma follow?
questions.explanation: Hereditary retinoblastoma is transmitted in an autosomal dominant pattern with incomplete penetrance, due to a germline mutation in the RB1 gene.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for advanced bilateral retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: In cases of advanced bilateral retinoblastoma, enucleation of the most affected eye is the treatment of choice for local control and to avoid dissemination.

questions.question: What histological finding is characteristic of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes are typical histological formations of retinoblastoma, consisting of tumor cells arranged around a central lumen.

questions.question: What is the most common age of presentation of bilateral hereditary retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Bilateral hereditary retinoblastoma is usually diagnosed before the age of 2 years, due to early detection by leukocoria or strabismus.

questions.question: What is the most common age of presentation of unilateral sporadic retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Unilateral sporadic retinoblastoma is usually diagnosed between 2 and 3 years of age, unlike bilateral hereditary forms that present earlier.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic genetic mutation in unilateral sporadic retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: In the unilateral sporadic form, the RB1 gene mutation occurs at the somatic level (not inherited), affecting only the tumor tissue, without germinal involvement.

questions.question: What is the main clinical sign of suspected retinoblastoma in a child?
questions.explanation: Leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is the most classic and frequent sign of retinoblastoma, especially in the unilateral form, and should always be ruled out in young children.

questions.question: What imaging test is the first choice to confirm retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Ocular ultrasound is the most accessible and useful initial test to detect intraocular masses with calcifications, a typical finding of retinoblastoma.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for advanced unilateral intraocular retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: For advanced unilateral intraocular retinoblastoma (groups D/E), enucleation remains the standard of treatment, as it allows definitive local control and prevents dissemination.

questions.question: What is the prognosis of unilateral sporadic retinoblastoma treated early?
questions.explanation: Unilateral sporadic retinoblastoma treated early has excellent survival (>95%), because it is usually detected before extraocular extension.

questions.question: What is the most common sign in the classic triad of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Leukocoria is the white pupillary reflex and constitutes the most frequent and characteristic sign of retinoblastoma, present in more than 60% of cases at diagnosis.

questions.question: Which finding in the classic triad indicates tumor retinal detachment?
questions.explanation: Leukocoria in retinoblastoma may be due to the tumor occupying the vitreous or causing retinal detachment, reflecting white light through the pupil.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of strabismus in the classic retinoblastoma triad?
questions.explanation: Strabismus in retinoblastoma occurs due to loss of central visual fixation due to tumor occupation of the macula or retinal detachment.

questions.question: What mechanism explains the red eye in the classic retinoblastoma triad?
questions.explanation: Red eye in advanced retinoblastoma is usually due to neovascular glaucoma secondary to retinal ischemia due to the tumor, with increased intraocular pressure.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT part of the classic retinoblastoma triad?
questions.explanation: The classic triad of retinoblastoma includes leukocoria, strabismus, and red eye; Nystagmus is a late and nonspecific finding, not considered part of the initial triad.

questions.question: What examination confirms the diagnosis of leukocoria in a child?
questions.explanation: Fundus examination under anesthesia is the diagnostic standard for retinoblastoma, allowing the intraocular tumor mass to be directly visualized and its extension evaluated.

questions.question: What is the typical age of presentation of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Retinoblastoma is the most common malignant intraocular tumor in childhood, with a mean age of diagnosis between 12 and 24 months, and more than 90% of cases occur before the age of 5 years.

questions.question: What funduscopic finding characterizes Coats disease?
questions.explanation: Coats disease is characterized by retinal telangiectasias with massive lipid exudation, unlike retinoblastoma, which usually presents a solid, whitish tumor mass.
hashtags: Shields JA, Shields CL. Intraocular Tumors. 3rd ed. 2016

questions.question: What is the main differential diagnosis of retinoblastoma in children?
questions.explanation: Coats disease is the main differential diagnosis of retinoblastoma, especially in males aged 6-8 years, as it also causes leukocoria and exudative retinal detachment.

questions.question: What imaging test confirms the presence of calcifications in retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: B-mode ocular ultrasound is highly sensitive in detecting intratumoral calcifications, a key finding that differentiates retinoblastoma from Coats' disease, where there are no calcifications.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for advanced intraocular retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Enucleation remains the treatment of choice for advanced intraocular retinoblastoma (groups D and E) when there is no possibility of preserving useful vision or tumor control with conservative therapies.

questions.question: What clinical feature helps differentiate retinoblastoma from Coats disease?
questions.explanation: Intratumoral calcifications are pathognomonic of retinoblastoma and are rarely observed in Coats' disease, where telangiectasias and lipid exudation without calcification predominate.
hashtags: Shields JA, Shields CL. Atlas of Intraocular Tumors. 2015

questions.question: Which ultrasound finding is pathognomonic of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Intratumoral calcifications on ocular ultrasound are highly suggestive of retinoblastoma, since other ocular tumors rarely present this finding.

questions.question: What is the most common malignant intraocular tumor in childhood?
questions.explanation: Retinoblastoma is the most common malignant intraocular tumor in children, originating from immature retinal cells, and usually presents with leukocoria.

questions.question: What classic clinical sign guides the diagnosis of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is the most characteristic clinical sign of retinoblastoma, although it can also appear in congenital cataracts or Coats' disease.

questions.question: Which imaging study confirms intratumoral calcifications in retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: B-mode ocular ultrasound is the method of choice to detect intratumoral calcifications, a key finding in the diagnosis of retinoblastoma.

questions.question: What is the first line of treatment for early-stage intraocular retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: In small and localized tumors, laser photocoagulation or cryotherapy can be effective as conservative treatment, avoiding enucleation.

questions.question: What gene is mutated in most hereditary retinoblastomas?
questions.explanation: The mutation of the RB1 tumor suppressor gene (chromosome 13q14) is the fundamental cause of hereditary retinoblastoma, following Knudson's two-hit theory.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for unilateral advanced intraocular retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: In advanced unilateral retinoblastoma (groups D/E), enucleation is the standard treatment for local control and to avoid dissemination.

questions.question: What stage of retinoblastoma warrants first-line chemotherapy?
questions.explanation: In groups B and C, systemic or intra-arterial chemotherapy is used to shrink the tumor and avoid enucleation, provided there is no extraocular invasion.

questions.question: What is the main objective of enucleation in retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Enucleation seeks to eliminate advanced intraocular tumor and prevent metastatic spread, although it sacrifices the eye and vision.

questions.question: What drug is frequently used in intra-arterial chemotherapy for retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Melphalan is the most commonly used agent in selective intra-arterial chemotherapy for retinoblastoma, with a high tumor control rate.

questions.question: When is chemotherapy contraindicated as the primary treatment of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Invasion of the optic nerve beyond the lamina cribrosa or extraocular disease contraindicates primary chemotherapy, requiring enucleation and adjuvant chemotherapy.

questions.question: Which finding in leukocoria suggests advanced retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Leukocoria due to retinoblastoma presents as a whitish or yellowish mass in the retina visible through the pupil, indicating advanced tumor.

questions.question: What is the maximum recommended tumor size for laser photocoagulation in retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Laser photocoagulation is indicated in small retinoblastomas, generally less than 3 mm in diameter, without extensive vitreous or subretinal involvement.

questions.question: What type of laser is typically used in photocoagulation of small retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: The argon laser is the most used in photocoagulation of small retinoblastoma, since its wavelength is selectively absorbed by the pigmented tumor tissue.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of laser photocoagulation in retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Laser photocoagulation acts through direct thermal destruction of tumor tissue, coagulating the vessels that nourish it and eliminating neoplastic cells.

questions.question: What complication is most common after laser photocoagulation for small retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Vitreous hemorrhage is the most common complication after laser photocoagulation in retinoblastoma, due to the rupture of tumor vessels during treatment.

questions.question: What criteria contraindicate laser photocoagulation as the sole treatment in retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: The presence of active vitreous seeding contraindicates laser photocoagulation as monotherapy, since the laser does not reach the tumor cells dispersed in the vitreous.

questions.question: How many laser photocoagulation sessions are usually required to treat a small retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Two to three laser photocoagulation sessions spaced every 3-4 weeks are usually required to achieve complete destruction of the small tumor.

questions.question: What is the most common primary tumor in patients with hereditary retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Osteosarcoma is the second most common primary tumor in patients with hereditary retinoblastoma, especially in areas of previous radiation, due to the RB1 gene mutation.
hashtags: Kleinerman RA, et al. J Clin Oncol. 2005

questions.question: What gene is involved in hereditary retinoblastoma and predisposition to second tumors?
questions.explanation: The germline mutation of the RB1 gene (13q14) is the cause of hereditary retinoblastoma and confers a high risk of second primary tumors such as osteosarcoma and melanoma.

questions.question: What factor increases the risk of second primary tumor in hereditary retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: External radiation therapy in patients with hereditary retinoblastoma significantly increases the risk of second primary tumors, especially osteosarcoma in the radiation field.

questions.question: What is the average age of onset of a second primary tumor in hereditary retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Second primary tumors in hereditary retinoblastoma usually appear in adolescence or early adulthood, with a peak between 10 and 20 years of age.

questions.question: What type of secondary tumor is characteristic in hereditary non-radiation-related retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Cutaneous melanoma is a second primary tumor described in patients with hereditary retinoblastoma even without exposure to radiotherapy, due to the genetic susceptibility of RB1.
hashtags: Fletcher O, et al. Br J Cancer. 2004

questions.question: What imaging study is recommended for surveillance of second bone tumors in hereditary retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Positron emission tomography (PET) is useful for the early detection of osteosarcoma and other second bone tumors in patients with high-risk hereditary retinoblastoma.

questions.question: What is the most common ocular finding in hereditary retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: In hereditary retinoblastoma, the presentation is usually bilateral and bilateral leukocoria is the most common finding due to germline mutations in the RB1 gene.

questions.question: What screening study is recommended in first-degree relatives of a child with retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Fundus examination under sedation is the standard for screening in first-degree relatives to detect early tumors, since it allows a complete exploration of the retina.

questions.question: What is the gene responsible for hereditary retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: Hereditary retinoblastoma is caused by mutations in the RB1 gene, a tumor suppressor gene located on chromosome 13q14, which follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern.

questions.question: In which age group does retinoblastoma occur most frequently?
questions.explanation: Retinoblastoma is the most common malignant intraocular tumor in childhood, typically diagnosed before the age of 5 years, with a mean presentation around 18 months.

questions.question: What is the main route of spread of retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: The spread of retinoblastoma occurs primarily through the optic nerve to the central nervous system, with invasion of the optic nerve being a critical prognostic factor.

questions.question: Which fundus finding is characteristic of advanced retinoblastoma?
questions.explanation: In advanced stages, retinoblastoma presents as a whitish or creamy retinal mass, often with calcifications, which can occupy the vitreous cavity and cause leukocoria.
